captions.text: The trouble with one-of-a-kind things  is that there's only one of them. Well,
that's going in the opening sentence hall of  fame, Yahtzee– SHUT UP. Seriously. Consider
the plight of the parent who takes a starry-eyed  child to Mount Rushmore and then, when they say,
"Gosh, I enjoyed seeing stone representations  of presidents' heads; when can we look at some
more?" has to say, "I'm sorry, darling. That's  all the stone president's heads that exist. Well.
Assuming the current one doesn't have some mass  produced and repurposed as currency." Oh, the
audible breaking of that child's heart, confronted  for the first time with the realization that all
glory is ephemeral. Now imagine that child growing  up, playing and really enjoying, oh, let's say for
the sake of example Disco Elysium. "Boy, that was  thought provoking, creative, and intelligent; I'd
sure like to play more games like that." What on  earth are we supposed to say to that poor little
bastard? Well, here comes Esoteric Ebb going,  "OH BOY! IT'S MY TICKET TO THE BIG LEAGUES."
"Hang on! What about Planescape: Torment; that  was an intelligent, isometric, writing-heavy RPG,
just in a D&D setting. Boy, that was a good game;  kinda wish there'd been another one of those,
too." And then Esoteric Ebb goes, "OH BOY!  TWO TICKETS TO THE BIG LEAGUES IN ONE DAY!"
In brief, Esoteric Ebb is an indie  isometric CRPG that is, some might say,
unashamedly knocking off Planescape: Torment and  Disco Elysium. Like, it lists them as inspirations
in the credits and everything, just under the  office photocopier. From Planescape: Torment
we take the D&D setting and starting with the main  character waking up on a mortuary slab in a large,
circular room, and from Disco we take pretty much  everything else—we're an adventuring detective
investigating a murder in a story isolated to a  single city, and all of our RPG stats have their
own personalities with which we converse  in internal dialogues. The main difference
being that, instead of having annihilated  our own personality with substance abuse,
the protagonist of Egotistic Egg has annihilated  their own personality by being annihilated,
and we've been resurrected thanks to D&D magic.  We are The Cleric, the lowest-ranking enforcer
of the local religious authority, tasked to  get to the bottom of the mysterious bombing
of a tea shop in a fantasy city as well as who  killed us to prevent us reaching the truth,
against the backdrop of the city being abuzz in  the runup to their first democratic election,
where virtually every character is in  part defined by their party affiliation.
And the competing parties are about what you'd  expect: there's the ultra-right-wing nationalists,
the pro-business libertarians, and there's  the nice, lovely party that wants to make
things better for the poor and the working man,  and who are obviously not going to fucking win.
The ongoing theme of Estonian Elbow is taking  the complex philosophy and political debate of
Disco Elysium and pushing it through the filter  of magical fantasy adventures. It reminds me
of Balatro in that it innovates by taking  something that started out as an evolution
or satire of a thing—card battlers in Balatro's  case and role-playing games in Disco's case—and
pulls it back towards the original thing.  So while Disco's anthropomorphized RPG stats
all represented aspects of a complex, real-world  personality—electrochemistry, conceptualization,
composure, and all that—Electric Edna brings  that concept back to the classic D&D six. And
what's conceptually hilarious right off the bat is  that even your fucking stats in this game have got
their own political affiliations. Like, Strength  is a blatant fascist and Dexterity is a weaselly
libertarian. Intelligence, I think, aspires to  be some kind of world-conquering evil genius,
which as a dedicated cryptic crossword  enjoyer makes me feel slightly called out.
The writing is obviously the strong point of  Enigmatic Echidna; the setting is well realized:
this semi-industrialized fantasy city built  around a mysterious ancient pillar like diverse,
multicolored pubes around a nurturing, patriarchal  todger. That's also sitting on the inevitable D&D
multilayered horror dungeon, which the townspeople  acknowledge the way people in the real world do
potholes and aggressive, swooping magpies, and  this is where the game stumbles a bit. With the
aspiration to be an interactive D&D campaign  as well as a Disco-style detective adventure,
the game is obliged to sprinkle in some thrilling  monster and trap encounters to simulate the moment
the DM is concerned the players are about to fall  asleep face-down in the Doritos. But there aren't
really any combat mechanics or indeed any  mechanics at all besides dialogue trees and
random stat checks, so you'll be blundering down  a dungeon corridor when the game'll go, "OH, SHIT!
A roper!" "Oh, shit! Do I survive?" "No! Reload!"  "Oh, shit! Do I survive?" "No! Reload!" "Oh, shit!
Do I survive?" "Yes, you did that time." "Right  you are!" Then you heal up with short rests,
spells, and the 900 apples and mushrooms you've  picked up, and with bugger all changed and no
lessons learned, you merrily march on straight  into a fucking gelatinous cube. Start again.
And none of this is adding much to the core  experience 'cause the core experience lies in
the dialogue and the characters, and there's not  much of a debate to be had with a gelatinous cube,
unless you count, "Ow, ow, ow, ow; Jesus  Christ, let me out, ow." On that front,
while the actually sentient characters can  sometimes lean a bit archetypal, as could be
expected in a setting and time when everyone's  wearing their politics on the sleeves of their
puffy renaissance fair doublets, there are plenty  of fun and unique personalities to interact with,
and I enjoyed the way the game explores  how the existence of magic messes with
the complexities of law and society in a  realistic, industrialized setting. I mean,
I play D&D—Adventure is Nigh season 5 coming  soon, Mortimer fans—and I recall once bringing
up with my colleagues while high on Mountain  Dew and laptop fumes that if you can resurrect
dead people with sufficiently valuable reagents,  then why don't a bunch of wizards set up a life
insurance company and laugh all the way to the  Ethereal Plane or whatever? And it was gratifying
to see questions like that being addressed in  the dense world building of Egregious Erotica.
I suppose the final question is how Eddie  the Eagle Edwards ultimately compares to
Disco Elysium, because if you're going to roll up  and piss in the same urinal as a game like that,
you're going to need a very big willy or at least  one with an interesting enough shape to spark
conversation. And on that front, it must be said  that Extreme Emasculation Event is a lot smaller
and a lot less clever. I mean—spoiler alert—it  ends with the protagonist visiting an astral realm
and getting everything explained to them by God,  which is the go-to plot device for a DM who has
to hastily wrap up 'cause his mum just honked her  horn outside. At the breakfast table of culture,
it is the teenager eating pancakes next to Disco  Elysium's stern dad reading his serious newspaper
with his manly porridge with salt in it  and everything. But I'm sure there are a
lot of people who might prefer the company of the  former, who found themselves intimidated by Disco
Elysium's scope and the way its pipe vibrates  as it reads a particularly salacious article,
and if that's you, and you prefer something  shorter and snackier with goblins in it and
that's generally a softer place to land, then you  might well enjoy Esoteric Ebb. There's a certain
gratification in seeing democracy working in a  fantasy setting. Actually, let me correct that
slightly: There's a certain gratification  in seeing democracy working SOMEWHERE.
